symptom: Registry creation fails with 400 error; DNS CNAME leaked by RP due to create failure, Vnet handshake failure, or subscription disable/delete during provisioning
rootCause: RP CNAME rollback has races due to the global nature of CNAME records and ARM retry policies, resulting in orphan CNAME records that prevent re-creation of the registry with the same name
solution: Query RPActivity Kusto for LoginServerName to identify CorrelationId of failed request. Use Geneva action 'Clean up orphan registry CNAME record' to manually clean up the leaked CNAME.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR DNS And Name Reservation

symptom: After deleting ACR in tenantA, CheckNameAvailability in tenantA shows Available but creating same-name registry in tenantB returns 'alreadyExists' or NameNotAvailable_NotAllowedByPolicy
rootCause: Name Reservation Service (NRS) reserves registry names for 30 days (standard), 180 days (Enterprise/CSP), or indefinitely (Internal) after deletion to prevent DNS sniping
solution: 1) Query RPActivity to confirm NRS reservation (ReserveNameAsync returns success). 2) Check CheckNameAvailabilityAsync logs for 'NameNotAvailable_NotAllowedByPolicy'. 3) Run ACIS action 'Force Delete Registry name from Name Reservation Service' with tenantId and subscriptionId from NRS logs. 4) Wait 1 hour; verify via NRS Kusto cluster (nrsdiagnostics.eastus.kusto.windows.net). 5) If failed, file ICM to 'Azure DNS Name Reservation'.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR DNS And Name Reservation

symptom: docker push to ACR from Docker Desktop (Windows) returns UNAUTHORIZED error: "authentication required, visit https://aka.ms/acr/authorization"; token server requests fail with 401; Kusto shows basic_credentials_invalid with 'Authorization header is not present'
rootCause: Windows Credential Manager (wincred) stores stale or corrupt ACR credentials; Docker Desktop uses wincred by default on Windows and fails to send valid credentials to the token API
solution: Remove stale ACR credentials from Windows Credential Manager: Start → Credential Manager → Windows Credentials → find ACR entry → delete it. Then retry docker push/pull.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Docker push from Docker desktop failing with unauthorized error

symptom: AKS pod fails to pull image from ACR with 'no route to host' error: 'dial tcp <private-ip>:443: connect: no route to host'; DNS correctly resolves ACR to private endpoint IP but connection fails
rootCause: ACR private endpoint connection is disconnected (connection was removed by the private-link resource owner); NIC Effective Routes shows incorrect routing to the private endpoint destination
solution: 1) Check NIC Effective Routes for the private endpoint IP to verify incorrect routing. 2) Check private endpoint connection status in Azure Portal — if 'Disconnected', the connection was removed. 3) Engage network team to investigate. 4) Recreate the private endpoint for ACR.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR FailedPullImage NoRouteToHost PE

symptom: Customer accidentally deleted images or repositories from ACR and needs to recover them; soft-delete not enabled or the image is older than 7-day soft-delete retention period
rootCause: ACR soft-delete policy has a default 7-day retention; images deleted beyond this window or without soft-delete enabled cannot be self-recovered by the customer
solution: 1) Query RegistryManifestEvent for DeleteRepository actions to confirm deletion and get ArtifactType. 2) If deleted < 30 days ago, query WorkerServiceActivity to get the tag/digest. 3) Confirm tag with customer if multiple tags. 4) Reach out to TA (Technical Advisor) to perform image/repository recovery.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Image or Repository Recovery

symptom: Customer needs more than 200 ACR Private Endpoints for their secure connectivity scenario; default limit of 200 is insufficient
rootCause: ACR Private Endpoint limit is 200 by design, constrained by ARM and geo-replication requirements for RP access consistency; Premier SKU only
solution: 1) Communicate limitations: no geo-replication CRUD after limit increase; max 10 registries per region in subscription. 2) Verify current registry count via RegistryMasterData Kusto query (must be ≤10 per region). 3) Open ICM S3 via ASC to ACR PG requesting new limit (201-1000) including Kusto output and customer confirmation. 4) After PG confirmation, verify with: az acr show-usage --name <registry-name> --query "value[?name=='PrivateEndpointConnections']" -o table
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Increase private endpoint limit

symptom: Customer needs to move ACR instance to a different AAD tenant while maintaining registry name and login server; Azure Portal incorrectly shows ACR as ineligible to move between tenants
solution: 见排查指南: guides/drafts/ado-wiki-a-acr-move-to-different-aad-tenant.md
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Move to different AAD Tenant

symptom: AKS pod pull from ACR fails with '401 Unauthorized' error even though customer credentials and RBAC access are valid; error appears as 'failed to fetch anonymous token: unexpected status: 401 Unauthorized' alongside a NotFound error
rootCause: AKS kubelet sends two parallel requests when accessing ACR: one with empty credentials (anonymous) and one with non-empty credentials. When pulling a non-existent image from ACR without anonymous access enabled, the authenticated request returns 404 (Not Found) but the anonymous request returns 401; kubelet surfaces both errors, making it appear as an auth failure
solution: Check whether the image actually exists in the ACR registry. The 401 is a misleading side-effect of AKS kubelet's anonymous-access retry logic — it is not a real authentication failure. If image exists, investigate actual auth; if image does not exist, inform customer the 401 is expected behavior when image is missing.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Pull Image 401 Anonymous Token

symptom: Image push to ACR succeeds with no error but the pushed image/artifact (e.g., Helm chart via OCI) has no content or is empty in the registry
rootCause: Azure Quarantine feature (automatically enabled when Microsoft Defender for Cloud is enabled) intercepts and blocks content from being committed to ACR; push appears to succeed but quarantine holds the content
solution: Disable quarantine policy on the ACR: 1) Get registry resource ID: id=$(az acr show --name <registry-name> --query id -o tsv). 2) Disable quarantine: az resource update --ids $id --set properties.policies.quarantinePolicy.status=disabled
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Push Failure no content

symptom: ACR geo-replication create or delete operation fails and gets stuck in 'Creating' or 'Deleting' state indefinitely
rootCause: Backend issue: metadata inconsistencies across regions, or missing permission/role assignment for ACR 1st party (1p) App on the customer TM subscription
solution: 1) Check ARM Incoming Request logs (ARMProd) by subscription/registry URI with httpMethod=delete/put to get clientRequestId. 2) Query ACR RPActivity by CorrelationId==clientRequestId to identify backend error message. 3) If permission missing for ACR 1p App, escalate to ACR PG oncall to add role assignment. Escalation paths: post in acr-sup Teams channel, or raise ICM to ACR PG team directly.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Replication CRUD TSG

symptom: Push or pull fails with 'unknown: The operation is disallowed on this registry, repository or image' error; registry has reached or is consistently trending toward the 40TiB storage limit
rootCause: ACR default maximum storage is 40TiB per registry; this is a hard design limit; no automatic cleanup occurs once the limit is reached
solution: 1) Verify storage trend: az acr show-usage -r {registryName}. 2) Advise customer: delete unused images, create retention policy for untagged manifests, enable auto-purge, enable soft-delete. 3) If increase needed: open ICM to ACR PG with subscription ID, registry name, and desired size (max 50TiB standard, 60TiB with special approval). Caveats: >40TiB = no SLA; >40TiB + new geo-replication = no SLA; cannot reduce limit once increased; internal hard limit is 60TiB.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Storage Limit Increase

symptom: ACR Tasks fail with 403 Forbidden after June 1st, 2025, when the networkRuleBypassAllowedForTasks policy flag is not explicitly set
rootCause: ACR Phase 2 behavior change (effective June 1st, 2025): network bypass for ACR Tasks is denied by default if the new 'networkRuleBypassAllowedForTasks' flag is not explicitly configured. Previously, existing network bypass settings were honored automatically.
solution: Option 1: Explicitly enable the new policy via `az acr update --name <registry-name> --set networkRuleBypassAllowedForTasks=true`. Option 2: Migrate to ACR Agent Pool with dedicated VNet access. Option 3: Run ACR purge/build locally using the ACR CLI binary from GitHub (https://github.com/azure/acr-cli).
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Tasks Network Bypass Policy

symptom: ACR Tasks fail with error: 'ACR Tasks requests for the registry are not supported since the subscription is using the free Azure credit payment plan'
rootCause: ACR temporarily paused ACR Task runs from subscriptions using Azure trial/free credits (including $200 USD Azure free credits, Azure for Students, etc.) due to credit reorganization policy.
solution: Upgrade the subscription to a paid plan to restore ACR Tasks access. For formal access requests where customers with free credits need ACR Tasks, create an IcM with ACR PG team providing: (1) workloads to run on ACR Tasks, (2) expected usage/load for ACR Tasks.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Tasks Temporary Pause

symptom: az acr login fails with 403 CONNECTIVITY_REFRESH_TOKEN_ERROR when connecting from a VM in a spoke VNet to ACR via private endpoint in a Virtual WAN Hub (Hub-and-Spoke) environment
rootCause: Azure Firewall in the Virtual Hub intercepts HTTPS traffic as a proxy and performs its own DNS resolution. Since Private DNS Zones cannot be linked to a Virtual Hub, Azure Firewall resolves ACR's FQDN to its Public IP. ACR then rejects the request because it originates from a public IP (firewall rules block public access).
solution: Create a new private endpoint for ACR in the same VNet as the source VM. Update the Private DNS Zone to point to this dedicated private endpoint. This ensures all traffic remains within the private network and bypasses the Virtual Hub Azure Firewall.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Unauthorized Access Errors with Virtual WAN Hub

symptom: VNET/Subnet from a different subscription added to ACR Firewall disappears after refreshing the page with no error message
rootCause: The Microsoft.ContainerRegistry Resource Provider is not registered in the second subscription where the VNET/Subnet resides. Without the RP registration, ACR cannot persist the cross-subscription network rule.
solution: 1) Log in to the VNET's subscription: `az login` then `az account set -s <sub_id>`. 2) Register the RP: `az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerRegistry`. 3) Re-add the VNET/Subnet to ACR Firewall via Portal or CLI and save.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Virtual Network & Firewall Configuration Cross Subscription

symptom: ACR webhook to App Service fails with 500 InternalServerError ('An error occurred') when the webhook endpoint is in a different subscription or behind an ILB ASE
rootCause: ACR webhooks to App Service Kudu endpoints in a different subscription are not supported. ACR cannot make outbound calls through a VNET to Kudu in a spoke model environment. ILB ASE does not have a public endpoint and can only be reached via the VNet.
solution: Use Azure DevOps pipelines for CI/CD deployments to App Service instead of ACR webhooks. Reference: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/app-service/environment/using-an-ase#publishing
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR Webhook fails to push to App Service

symptom: AKS pods fail to pull images from ACR with 'unauthorized: authentication required'; cluster uses Managed Identity for Kubelet; no imagePullSecrets configured
rootCause: The Kubelet managed identity is missing the AcrPull role assignment on ACR. The automatic role assignment during `az aks update --attach-acr` may have failed or was not performed with sufficient AAD permissions (Owner or Contributor+UAA required).
solution: Option 1 (preferred): Detach and re-attach ACR to AKS cluster using `az aks update -n <cluster> -g <rg> --detach-acr <acr>` then `az aks update -n <cluster> -g <rg> --attach-acr <acr>` (requires Owner or Contributor+UAA). Option 2: Manually find the Kubelet managed identity via `az aks show --query identityProfile.kubeletidentity.resourceId` and add AcrPull role in ACR IAM portal.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR authentication with AKS deployed using Managed Identity

symptom: ACR login fails and image pulls are blocked; ACR reports as disabled; attempting to re-enable the admin user also fails with 'ACR disabled'
rootCause: ARM maintenance during a registry update operation caused an inconsistency between ARM's state and the ACR resource provider. This led the RP to set the registry as disabled, blocking docker login and pull operations (which require credential retrieval from the ACR service).
solution: Run Kusto query on armprodgbl cluster to confirm 404 ARM responses around the time of the issue. Then open an IcM with ACR PG Triage to review and correct the Master Entity for the registry. Kusto query: cluster('armprodgbl.eastus').database('ARMProd').Unionizer('Requests','HttpIncomingRequests') | where operationName endswith '/MICROSOFT.CONTAINERREGISTRY/REGISTRIES/' | where httpStatusCode == 404 | distinct registryName
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR disabled issue

symptom: ACR repository tags or images disappear unexpectedly; unclear whether caused by retention policy or external CI/CD automation
solution: 见排查指南: guides/drafts/ado-wiki-a-acr-image-deletion-investigation.md
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR image deletion investigation

symptom: AKS Service Connector operator image pull fails with 'not found' error referencing a specific old tag (e.g., sc-operator:20250417.1); SC extension is present in Extensions + applications but remains failed
rootCause: The Service Connector (SC) AKS extension does not auto-update. The installed version (e.g., 0.1.0) was hard-pinned to an image tag that has since been removed from ACR (scaksextension.azurecr.io/prod/image/sc-operator:<removed-tag>). kubelet repeatedly attempts to pull the removed tag and ACR returns NotFound.
solution: 1) Delete the outdated SC extension: `az k8s-extension delete --name <extension-name> --cluster-name <aks-cluster> --resource-group <rg> --cluster-type managedClusters`. 2) Recreate the Service Connection (e.g., Key Vault): `az aks connection create keyvault --connection <name> --resource-group <rg> --name <cluster> --target-resource-group <rg-of-kv> --vault <kv-name> --enable-csi --client-type none`. This re-adds the SC extension pinned to a valid current tag.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR image pulls fail service connector extension not auto updating

symptom: Azure Linux function app deployment fails with 'manifest invalid' and 'unknown blob' errors when pulling images from ACR behind private endpoint with public access disabled and 'Pull Image Over Vnet' enabled
rootCause: Known limitation: 'Pull Image Over Vnet' for premium and dedicated Linux functions only supports Docker manifest v2 format (application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json). OCI image index format (application/vnd.oci.image.index.v1+json) is not supported. OCI support is planned for H1 2025.
solution: Rebuild and push the container image using Docker manifest v2 format (application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json) instead of OCI image index format. Verify the manifest type before pushing.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR image pulls fail unknown blob

symptom: ACR image pull fails with 403 Forbidden despite AKS LB outbound IPs being allowed in ACR selected-network rules. Kusto logs show ACR is receiving requests from private IPs of AKS nodes rather than the expected public LB outbound IPs. Other AKS clusters in different subscriptions pull successfully.
rootCause: A VNet Service Endpoint for Microsoft.ContainerRegistry exists on the AKS cluster subnet. Service Endpoints force traffic to ACR through the Azure backbone network using the node's private IP, bypassing the LB outbound NAT. ACR's selected-network rules only allow the LB outbound public IPs, so requests arriving from private IPs are rejected with 403 Forbidden.
solution: Delete the VNet Service Endpoint for Microsoft.ContainerRegistry from the AKS cluster's subnet in the Azure Portal or via CLI. Once removed, ACR traffic uses the expected public outbound LB IP which matches the ACR allowed-network rules, resolving the 403 Forbidden error.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR imagepull forbidden serviceendpoint

symptom: ACR private link connectivity issues — pull failures, DNS resolution failures, or access errors when using private endpoints.
solution: 见排查指南: guides/drafts/ado-wiki-a-acr-private-link-troubleshooting-questions.md
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR private link troubleshooting questions

symptom: ACR retention policy for untagged manifests is enabled and configured, but untagged manifests remain in the repository and are not purged as expected. The retention policy runs but some manifests are never deleted.
rootCause: ACR retention policy does not support MediaType v1 manifests or OCI image indexes (e.g., application/vnd.oci.image.index.v1+json). Images pushed via `az acr import` use the OCI image index media type which is not supported by the retention policy engine. Only MediaType v2 manifests are purged correctly.
solution: Update Docker/BuildKit to the latest version to avoid pushing v1 manifests. Avoid using `az acr import` for images where retention policy cleanup is required, as it pushes using unsupported OCI image index media type. Use Kusto query on RegistryManifestEvent to verify MediaType of affected manifests and confirm purge behavior via RPActivity table.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR retention policy for untagged manifests does not work as expected

symptom: Deleting a repository from ACR via Azure Portal returns a NAME_UNKNOWN error. The deleted repository still appears in Portal. Azure CLI returns 'Error: repository name not known to the registry' when attempting deletion.
rootCause: Azure Portal caches repository metadata and the stale cache persists after deletion until it expires. Additionally, if ACR image locks are configured on images in the repository, the delete operation is silently blocked and the repository remains.
solution: 1) Wait for the Azure Portal cache to expire and refresh naturally. 2) Check and remove any ACR image locks (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-registry/container-registry-image-lock) that may be blocking deletion. 3) If the delete is believed to have silently failed and metadata is inconsistent, escalate to ACR PG to run a Geneva Action to resync ARM metadata with the actual registry state.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR unable to delete repository

symptom: Untagged images in ACR continue to appear in Microsoft Defender for Cloud vulnerability reports under 'Azure registry container images should have vulnerabilities resolved', even after the images have been untagged and are no longer visible in repository tag listings.
rootCause: Untagging an ACR image only removes the tag reference; the underlying image manifest and all associated layers remain in the registry. Defender for Cloud scans all manifests in the registry (not just tagged ones), so untagged manifests continue to generate vulnerability alerts.
solution: 1) List all untagged manifests: `az acr manifest list-metadata --registry <ACR_NAME> --name <REPO_NAME> --query "[?tags==null].digest" -o tsv`. 2) Delete them using acr purge: `PURGE_CMD="acr purge --filter 'hello-world:.*' --untagged --ago 1d" && az acr run --cmd "$PURGE_CMD" --registry myregistry /dev/null`. 3) Or enable ACR retention policy to automatically purge untagged manifests.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR untagged images showing in defender

symptom: ACR webhook creation fails with error 'You do not have access for this operation, please contact the owner of this registry.' even when the user has Owner role assigned on the ACR resource.
rootCause: The Azure Portal creates webhooks via an ARM (Azure Resource Manager) deployment, not a direct API call to ACR. This deployment mechanism requires additional subscription-level permissions (Microsoft.Resources/deployments/* and Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups/read) beyond the Owner role on the ACR resource itself.
solution: Create a custom RBAC role with the required permissions and assign it to the user at the subscription level in addition to their ACR Owner role. Required permissions: Microsoft.Resources/deployments/read, Microsoft.Resources/deployments/write, Microsoft.Resources/deployments/validate/action, Microsoft.Resources/deployments/operations/read, Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups/read, Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups/deployments/read, Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups/deployments/operationstatuses/read. Create via: `az role definition create --role-definition acrwebhook.json`
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/ACR webhook creation permission issue

symptom: AKS nodes fail to pull images from ACR. Image pull errors occur when AKS attempts to create new pods. Kusto logs show node authentication failures against ACR.
rootCause: Two common causes: (1) The Service Principal (SP) credentials used by AKS have expired. (2) The SP configured in AKS kubelet identity does not match the SP that has ACR Pull role assigned in ACR IAM.
solution: (1) Check SP expiry: `az ad sp credential list --id <Service Principal ID>`. If expired, reset credentials: `az ad sp credential reset --name <SP> [--password <pwd>] [--years <n>]`. Find the SP secret used by nodes via `cat /etc/kubernetes/azure.json` on a cluster node. (2) Check AKS SP: `az aks show -n <cluster> -g <rg> | grep client`. Ensure this SP has AcrPull role assigned in ACR IAM. Redeploy pods after fixing SP credentials or IAM assignment.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/AKS Nodes Failing To Pull Images From Azure Container Registry

symptom: AKS cluster already integrated with ACR via `az aks update --attach-acr` fails to pull ACR images with errors: EOF, Forbidden, or 401 Unauthorized on token exchange requests to ACR oauth2/exchange endpoint. `az aks check-acr` reports 'ACR rejected token exchange: failed to send token exchange request: Forbidden'.
rootCause: AKS is behind a proxy server or firewall that is blocking outbound requests to ACR login server and its regional data endpoints. The Managed Identity token exchange requests to ACR cannot reach the registry endpoints.
solution: Allow the following ACR endpoints in the proxy server or firewall rules: `https://<acr>.azurecr.io` (login server / REST endpoint), `https://<acr>.<region>.data.azurecr.io` (data endpoint), `https://*.blob.core.windows.net` (layer storage). Reference: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-registry/container-registry-firewall-access-rules#allow-access-to-rest-and-data-endpoints
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/AKS is unable to pull ACR images

symptom: Azure Container Apps creation fails with ContainerAppOperationError 'Failed to provision revision for container app. Error details: Operation expired.' after 15-20 minutes, when the ACR registry name used is too long (e.g., 32 characters).
rootCause: Known bug in Azure Container Apps: when the associated ACR registry name exceeds a certain length (approximately 30 characters), the Container App provisioning operation times out and fails. Engineering team was tracking a fix.
solution: As a workaround, create a new ACR registry with a shorter name (under 30 characters) and use that registry for the Container App. Re-create the Container App pointing to the registry with the shorter name.

symptom: az acr build fails with "denied: client with IP X.X.X.X is not allowed access" when ACR has public network access disabled and pipeline runs on Azure DevOps self-hosted agents (even if agent is inside the same VNet as the ACR private endpoint)
rootCause: az acr build uses ACR Tasks infrastructure which runs on Azure-managed compute, not on the self-hosted DevOps agent. The agent only triggers the command; the actual build executes on ACR task compute that egresses via public Azure IP ranges, which is blocked when public network access is disabled.
solution: Option A (recommended, strict private-only): Create an ACR Dedicated Agent Pool injected into the VNet: az acr agentpool create --registry <acrName> --name <poolName> --tier S2 --subnet-id $subnetId. Then add --agent-pool <poolName> to az acr build. Option B (if dedicated pool unavailable in region): Allowlist AzureContainerRegistry service tag IPs in ACR firewall: az network list-service-tags --location <region> --query "values[?id == 'AzureContainerRegistry.<Region>'].properties.addressPrefixes[]" -o tsv, then add those IP ranges to the ACR network rules.
sourceRef: Supportability/AzureContainers/Containers Wiki:/Azure Kubernetes Service Wiki/ACR/TSG/az acr build with Private ACR using Azure DevOps SelfHosted Agents
